source_text: sources/comments/files/20251205-5247__Oncor-Electric-Delivery-Company-LLC/Oncor Reply Comments on Large Load Interconnection ANOPR.txt
overall_summary: Oncor takes no position on the merits of the ANOPR but opposes expansion of FERC jurisdiction to ERCOT entities that are not federal public utilities. The filing argues that any rule relying on FPA sections 201, 205, or 206 should not apply to ERCOT Entities, and criticizes commenters who have invoked FPA section 215 (reliability authority) as a basis for imposing large load interconnection rules on ERCOT. Oncor defends ERCOT's independence and regulatory model as beneficial to policy experimentation and market design.

quotes.text: Oncor therefore takes no position at this time on the merits of the proposed reforms or the Commission's authority under FPA sections 201, 205, or 206. However, Oncor urges the Commission to reject the invitations from certain commenters to use this proceeding as a vehicle for unprecedented assertions of authority over the Electric Reliability Council of Texas, Inc. (ERCOT) and its members.
quotes.concern: Opposes unprecedented expansion of FERC jurisdiction to ERCOT

quotes.text: any rule that relies on the Commission's authority over transmission and wholesale sales of electric energy in interstate commerce by public utilities under FPA sections 201, 205, and 206 should not apply to ERCOT or market participants operating solely within ERCOT that are not public utilities under FPA section 201(e) (ERCOT Entities).
quotes.concern: Rules under FPA 201/205/206 should exclude non-jurisdictional ERCOT entities

quotes.text: Chevron provides no explanation for how that section could lawfully be used to impose a new co-located load interconnection process on entities like ERCOT and ERCOT Entities that are not public utilities subject to plenary FERC jurisdiction with respect to transmission and wholesale sales of electric energy in interstate commerce.
quotes.concern: Section 215 reliability authority cannot lawfully extend to non-jurisdictional ERCOT entities

quotes.text: FERC should reject Chevron's request that it invoke its authority under section 215 to reinforce the legal basis of actions that the Commission may take in this proceeding. Doing so would only cause confusion about the scope of the reforms and raise unnecessary legal challenges.
quotes.concern: Invoking section 215 would create legal confusion and exposure

quotes.text: independence from plenary FERC oversight is a key part of ERCOT's unique regulatory model and allows for beneficial experimentation in policy implementation and market design. This model has helped Texas emerge as a leader in establishing new regulatory paradigms for interconnecting and serving large load customers.
quotes.concern: ERCOT independence enables beneficial regulatory experimentation

quotes.text: the Commission should carefully define key terms (including transmission system) and avoid unnecessary references to provisions like section 215 that apply to entities that are not jurisdictional public utilities.
quotes.concern: Key terms should be carefully defined to exclude non-jurisdictional entities

bins.name: FERC jurisdiction limits over ERCOT
bins.description: Oncor opposes any expansion of FERC authority to ERCOT entities that are not federal public utilities under FPA section 201(e). The filer argues that rules based on FERC's FPA 201/205/206 authority over public utilities and transmission should not apply to ERCOT or ERCOT-only market participants.

bins.name: FPA section 215 reliability authority should not extend to ERCOT
bins.description: Oncor opposes invoking FPA section 215 as the legal foundation for large load interconnection process rules that would reach ERCOT. The filing notes that section 215 is properly limited to reliability standards for bulk-power system operation and is distinct from the Commission's FPA 201/205/206 authority. Oncor acknowledges section 215 may have value for addressing large load impacts on reliability through NERC standards, but argues it cannot lawfully serve as the basis for interconnection process mandates on non-jurisdictional ERCOT entities.

bins.name: ERCOT independence enables beneficial regulatory experimentation
bins.description: Oncor supports ERCOT's independence from plenary FERC oversight, citing it as a key feature of ERCOT's regulatory model that enables beneficial policy experimentation and market design innovation. Texas has used this independence to become a leader in large load interconnection approaches.

bins.name: Careful definition of key terms to preserve jurisdictional boundaries
bins.description: Oncor supports careful definition of key terms like transmission system and recommends avoiding unnecessary references to provisions that apply to non-jurisdictional entities, in order to maintain clear jurisdictional boundaries.
